FOREWORD

The scope and general character of Percy Grainger's collection of music by other composers may be understood by reference to two things: firstly, his aims for the Grainger Museum and its collections; secondly, the circumstances of his career as a professional musician (performer, conductor and concert-giver).

In establishing the 'Music Museum and Grainger Museum' as it was originally called, Grainger wished to emphasise the creative rather than the executant side of music, to throw light upon the processes of musical composition as distinct from performances. Within this general aim, a geographical frame is provided by his particular championship of the so-called 'Nordic Group' of composers: British, Irish, American, Australian, Scandinavian. The chronological frame is roughly the period covered by his own lifetime: 1882-1961. The preference is for unknown or less-known compositions.

In pursuit of this end, Grainger asked composer friends to donate music manuscripts and published editions of their own works to the Museum. Cyril Scott, Balfour Gardiner, Roger Quilter and Herman Sandby, Grainger's fellow-students at Frankfurt, were the chief contributors. But in the course of a long and varied musical career Grainger attracted numerous gifts of published and manuscript music from composers whom he met as he travelled about in the United States of America, Europe and Australia. Some of these composers are still remembered today; others are now forgotten. The inscriptions bear witness to the range of his acquaintance and activities, the variety of motives for the gifts. Finally, there is that music which Grainger collected because he admired it, was interested in it or wished to promote it. In this latter category one might also include his assemblage of largely ephemeral popular vocal, piano and band music of the early twentieth century.

It was also part of Grainger's intention that the Museum's collections should demonstrate the way in which the creative personality is moulded by what he called 'culturizing influences': propitious circumstances or fructifying personalities. Accordingly, the private collections of friends and relations, including his father John Grainger and his manager Antonia Sawyer, find their place in his collection. He believed that these collections reflected the musical tastes of the donors and that, as he was influenced by his friends and relations, their tastes must have some bearing on the formation of his own musical preferences.

Grainger incorporated the above material into his personal collection of piano, orchestral, vocal, choral and band music; a diverse collection ranging from standard repertoire items which he annotated and used for performing and teaching purposes through to the complimentary review copies with which he was deluged by music publishers.
vi

Grainger regarded his collection of music by other composers as
one of the most significant collections in the Grainger Museum.
It is, as he himself has written of the whole Museum, the product
of one man's taste and criticism - his own - and is limited
accordingly. Nonetheless, by virtue of the composers represented,
it provides a fascinating field of study not merely of the
creative achievements of a particular group of composers, as
Grainger intended, but also of the changing character of music-
making and changes in private and public taste.

Kay Dreyfus
Curator, Grainger Museum
September 1983
vii

EXPLANATORY NOTE AND ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
_____________________________________

Classification
______________

The letter prefix "MG" has been given to all items in the Percy Grainger Music Collection to distinguish music from other categories of material in the Grainger collection. For music listed in this catalogue, the letter C has been added to indicate that it is written by composers other than Grainger himself. The more specific designations D,F,J,Q,R or S have been used for items in the sub-collections given to Grainger by Mrs Dahl, Winifred Francis, John Grainger, Aunty Jack, Hamilton Russell and Antonia Sawyer respectively.

Material in this catalogue is further classified according to type: published music, manuscripts, photoprints, popular music, or chamber music published by the Society for the Publication of American Music. This is indicated by the numbers 1 to 5 adjacent to the letter prefix.

The letter prefixes and numerals denoting type of material appear before a diagonal stroke. Letters after the stroke identify the composer. Usually these are the first three letters of the composer's surname but fewer or more letters have been used where necessary to create a unique code for each composer. The number separated by a hyphen from the composer code indicates a specific title by that composer. Further numbering is used only to differentiate various editions, arrangements, types of score etc. for that particular title.

Any final number preceded by a colon signifies that the item is a part or set of parts.

Cataloguing
___________

The catalogue has generally been prepared in accordance with the second edition of Anglo-American Cataloguing Rules1 and the descriptions therefore conform to the basic principles of the General International Standard Bibliographic Description. The second level of description, schematically illustrated in Rule 1.0D2, has been adopted and usually consists of the following elements, each preceded by specific punctuation:

TITLE
=  PARALLEL TITLES IN OTHER LANGUAGES
:  ADDITIONAL TITLE INFORMATION
/  COMPOSER
;  OTHER PERSON(S) RESPONSIBLE FOR THE WORK
. -  EDITION
. -  PLACE OF PUBLICATION
:  PUBLISHER
,  DATE OF PUBLICATION
. -  PHYSICAL DESCRIPTION
     including pagination and ; size
viii

. - (SERIES ; NUMBER WITHIN SERIES)
. : NOTES
    including medium of performance, language of text,
    duration of performance, plate number or publishers
    numbers, etc.

The indexes offer three points of access to Grainger's
Collection of Music by Other Composers.

In addition to those individuals responsible for the 'author-
ship' of each composition, any other person(s) associated in
any way with Grainger's own particular copy of that composition
are listed in the NAME INDEX.

All titles, including individual contents of larger works,
such as volumes of songs, and original language entries where
a piece was published in translation, are included in the
TITLE INDEX. Initial numerals which are not a distinctive
part of the title have been inverted, for filing purposes, in
this index.

The SUBJECT INDEX, based on headings assigned to each item
from Library of Congress Subject Headings2, offers an approach
for those interested primarily in genre or instrumentation.

The APPENDICES separate out items which are particularly note-
worthy, usually because of the attention they attracted from
Grainger himself.

1. Anglo-American Cataloguing Rules. 2nd ed. (London : The
   Library Association, 1978).
2. Library of Congress Subject Headings. 8th ed. (Washington :
   Library of Congress, 1975).

The initial stages of work on this catalogue were funded by a
generous grant from the Utah Foundation. Subsequent work was
funded by grants from the Victorian Ministry for the Arts and
by the Research and Graduate Studies Committees of the Faculty
of Music and the University of Melbourne. The publication of
this catalogue has been assisted by the Publications Sub-
Committee of the Committee on Research and Graduate Studies
of the University of Melbourne. The catalogue is photo-
reproduced from the manuscript typed by Sheena Weaver, with
assistance from Joan Burnham. Printed in Australia by
Associated Printers, Moorabbin.

my Hardanger Fiddle, so I give it to you! - The
high tones made him howl! - The music &

Sandby 1933.

Alpaert's 75th birthday. - Encl.: 2 leaflets,
in French, English, German & Italian, describing

BACH                                         15                                         BACH

Leipzig : Bartholf Senff, [1879]. - 15p. ;

annotations by Grainger. - Grainger's
classification no.: W53-9. - Cover bears stamp
of B. Firnberg. Musikalien-Handlung, Frankfurt
a. Main. - Note in Grainger's red ink holograph
encl.: W53-9 Bach-Brahms Chaconne, arr. for left
hand. I played this (also in Cons. concert)/when
my right hand was bad after/cutting off the top
of my 1st finger, cleaning my bicycle chain (at
Grüneburgweg, baker's)/(?). - This was when I
modelled a bust (after-/wards lost) of mother
with my left hand. No/doubt this period tended
to overstrengthen my L.H.

MG CI/BAC-68-1

[Das wohltemperirte Clavier]

Das wohltemperirte Klavier / von Joh. Seb. Bach ; revidirt und mit fingersatz versehen von Carl Czerny und F.A. Roitzsch. - Leipzig : Peters, [18--]. - 99p. ; 31cm. - Pl.no.: 2636, 2636. - T.p. inscribed in ink: To Percy Grainger/from/Louis Pabst/Melbourne/May 1893. - Grainger's holograph note in red ink, slipped inside cover, reads: W53-1/Louis Pabst's gift of Bach: Das Wohl. Klavier (Peters) to P.G. May 1893./The pieces marked with P (showing I had studied them with him are: 1st book, P I, PF(?) II, P & F V, P VI, P XI, P XVI, P XVII, P XXI. 2nd book, P & F XV. The fingering in the/ above are almost certainly by Pabst, who (I think) fingered everything for me/before I studied it. The one I loved most of all in Australia, P I in 2nd book, I did not study with anyone. Other P & F marked in these vols. were worked at/with Miss Burkitt or with Kwast in Frankfurt.

Cooke, editor of The Etude, to Grainger. It
reads in part: ... these new works/from the pen
of Mrs. Beach will be gladly welcomed by/

receipt of this letter & the five/compositions./
F.E.M. (Frederick E. Morse). - Transferred from

MG C1/CHO-20

[Preludes, piano]

Preludes and rondos / Fr. Chopin. - Leipzig :
Peters, [18--]. - 104p. ; 31cm. - (Edition
Peters) (His sämmtliche Pianoforte-Werke /
kritisch revidirt und mit fingersatz versehen
von Herrmann Scholtz). - Contents: 24 préludes,
op. 28 - Prélude op. 45 - Rondo (op. 1) C
minor - Rondo à la Mazur - Rondo (op. 6) Eb
major. - Pl.no.: 6218. - Op. 28, no. 17
classified W53-8 by Grainger & a note in his
red ink holograph is slipped between p.30 &
p.31: W 53-8 Chopin Prelude No. 17 (in complete
Preludes, Peters), fingered & adapted for my
small hands by Pabst,/& marked P at end by him,
showing he had taught it to me. (The sost. pedal
marks are mine & Lotta's, around 1920)/I
admired Pabst tremendously for showing me Bach
for the first time, but when he gave me this
Prelude 17,&/Grieg's "Norweg. Bridal Procession",
he lost caste in my eyes. "I shall never think
the same of him again", I said to mother. I saw
his artistic taste was not reliable, if he could
approve also of this Prelude./I was very stern
at the age of 11-12. Pabst said to mother: "We
mustn't let him grow so one-sided" "(liking only/
Bach).

April 15, 1942

                (To Nathaniel Dett, Negro composer of "Juba Dance",
                 "Music in the Mine", "The Ordering of MOSES")

Dear friend Nathaniel,

                When I was last in White Plains (just for a few
hours) I gave myself the pleasure of looking at your piano suite (was
the title "Tropic Night", or similar title?) & rejoiced at the richness
of the texture, the personal & individual quality of the harmonies, etc. -
as always in your work.  Nevertheless I must confess that no piano
music is real or alive to me.  I find it utterly impossible (I always
have) to take piano music seriously.  This is proved (I think) by the
fact that, altho I am a professional pianist, I have never (since I was
about 16) composed for piano (for I don't call transcriptions of Tchai-
kowsky's "Flower Waltz" & Richard Strauss "Rosebearer" Love duet "composi-
tions").  All my Grainger piano publications are originally written for
chorus? or chambermusic, or band, or orchestra; then "dished up" (re-
hashed) for piano.  (Think how practical it would have been for me to have
a Piano Concerto of my own!  But it seemed to me, as an Australian, that
to "star" one performer, out of 70 or 100, was most undemocratic.)
To me music is melody, whether simple line melody, as in folksong, or
harmonic melodiousness, as in Bach, Delius, etc.  I not only loathe
piano music but all the other compromise instruments that foreran the piano
(lute, guitar, harpsichord, etc.), in which one player (for convenience
as a conductor, or for accompaniment purposes) sums up the whole harmonic
texture that was conceived as polyphonic melody.  I cannot for the life
of me see why that should be considered a full artistic utterance, when it
is merely (as I see it) a most teasing & defrauding compromise.  Why
geniuses like you, Bach, Grieg can be willing to compose for keyboard
instruments I shall never understand.

                You must have thought me a very poor colleague (a disap)pointing
fellow composer) all these years - playing your "Juba Dance", but none of
your other piano pieces.  I played "Juba Dance" because I sensed it would
become a best seller & because I thought it is always rare that a real
genius like you are able to write a best seller - when it happens, one
should (if one can) help the composer to "cash in" on it.  Your choral
works (which ARE real to me) I have always talked & written about all I
can, because I not only know them to be works of great genius, but also
because they are part of my own soul life.

                If I had been a "natural pianist" (liking my instrument & memorising
easily) I would have played lots of your piano pieces (as those of Cyril
Scott, J.A. Carpenter & other geniuses that I admire) - in spite of dis-
liking the piano.  But having such difficulty in my piano career (such
dislike of my instrument, such difficulty in memorizing) my problem has
always been: How can I (being a composer & needing some of my time for
composing) keep up my pianistic career & yet learn as few pieces as possib-
ble?  It has been a great problem: a wretched life.  But better than star-
ving, of course.

                I wish I might see you, dear friend & genius!
                Warm greetings to you and yours,
                         Yours ever,

                         Percy.
DETT

MG Cl/GAR-21-1

Michaelchurch / by H. Balfour Gardiner. -
London : Forsyth, cl923. - 15p. ; 36cm. - For
piano. - Dedication: To Percy Grainger.

MG Cl/GAR-21-2

Michaelchurch / by H. Balfour Gardiner ... 2nd
copy. - Cover inscribed: 27.10.23/To Percy,/
with all good wishes/H. Balfour Gardiner. -
Copy stitched into cardboard cover. - Some
annotations for performance and copy of part of
a Gardiner letter, in Grainger's autograph, on
p. 6; Balfour Gardiner writes, Dec. 11, 1923:/
"It is the passage at the bottom of p.6 of
Michaelchurch I have/lengthened: that was
entirely owing to your suggestion, for which I
was most/grateful. I wish I could have done
more - I remember one day going quite mad
about/those trampling chords, wandering off
into remote keys piling up masses on masses of/
sound. If only one could realise those ideals!
but I, at least, run on a very short chain, &
am always/dragged back by petty practical
considerations".

MG CI/LON-1

Six king's daughters - William Hall. - 2 copies
(1 with paper cover, 1 with board cover). -
Grainger's ink holograph note (1 leaf) on
Cromwell Place letterhead slipped inside cover
copy 1: This copy of Brockway's "Lonesome
Tunes", given to me by Lotta Hough shortly
after/they appeared, was often used by/my
beloved mother, & was probably one of the very
last (perhaps even the last) music books she
played from.
She played the piano accompaniments, while/I
played the tunes on my saxophone or sax-
ophone, & the songs we used most/were: Brother
Green/William Hall/
In other ways, also, a most sacred/relic to me.

MG CI/SCA-1

[Harpsichord music]

Opere complete per clavicembalo / di Domenico Scarlatti ; criticamente rivedute e ordinate in forma di suites da Alessandro Longo. - Milano : Ricordi, [s.a.]. - 6v. ; 31cm. - V.1-6 each contain 50 pieces divided into 10 suites. - Pl.no.: 104918-104923. - V.1 signed by Grainger on cover & t.p. - T.p. also bears inscription: 08.

MG CI/SCA-2

[Harpsichord music. Selections]

Achtzen ausgewählte Klavierstücke / von Domenico Scarlatti ; in Form von Suiten gruppirt, kritisch bearbeitet und mit einem Vorwort, herausgegeben von Hans von Bülow. - Leipzig : Peters, [Vorwort 1864]. - 23p. ; 30cm. - Pl.no.: 4458-60. - Encl.: red ink note in Grainger's autograph; W53-6 Pabst's bound edition of Scarlatti-v.Bülow Klavierstücke (N. Kymmel, Buchhandlung, Riga), given/to P.G. just before Pabst left Melbourne. The ones fingered in Pabst's hand & marked at the end P (meaning/he had studied or taught them, I suppose) are Suite I, no. 6; Suite II, no. 1 & 4 (the latter Bourrée) & 6./The only one I remember working at is the Bourée & I simply smells of the last times in Melbourne, before/ leaving for F'furt. Did I work at it with Pabst or with Miss Burkitt? Is "91" on page 1 the numbering of Pabst's musical library (At Riga?)?

The 2 vols. originally wrapped in cover
inscribed by Grainger: Songs of the North
(Scotish [sic] Folksongs) gift from Karl
Klimsch to P.G. Frankfurt a/M. These volumes
(when Klimsch showed them to me around 1896-
1898) where [sic] the first examples of Folk-
songs settings I remember having come in
contact with (I mean sung folksongs - I had
heard Hungarian Dances, Spanish Dances before).

215                                                            WHITE

"12/Etuder" op. 60 & Carl Nielsen's/Chaconne
for piano/op 32/was (the here-enclosed) Card
from Frøken (Miss) Alette Schon/(very old
Danish friend of mother's & mine)/with
following in-writ [inscription]:/(Miss Skov is
nearly 90 & almost blind; hence her hard-to-
read-writing)/
Koere, koere Percy! Jeg toænker meget paa/
[Dear, dear Percy! I think much about/
[Dem og Ella. Tag vel imod vor gamle, elskede
[you & Ella. Receive well our old, beloved
[Weyse (1774-/1844) og vor (?afladte?) koere
Weyse (1774-/1844) & our (?deceased?) dear
Carl Nielsen./

This is a gift for my birthday, July 8, 1935/
Above No. 4 of the Weyse studies Miss Skov has
written/"Denne eskede vi i H.C. Andersen's
Tidsalder meget højt"/(This one we loved very
much in the period of H.C. Anderson)/(Hans
Christian)/She has always tried to win me for
the Nielsen Chaconne, but I/think it is bunk.
P.G.

of Linc. Posy as soon/as they were written.
Ella &I went to his summer school at/
Saugerties, NY several times./Her Love at 1st
sight was done first/there. All the same, I
couldn't/understand how the kind of man/he was
could have composed the/musical cornet solos he
had. Later/Leidzen told me Williams had paid/L.
to compose them, putting his/(W's) name to

220
221


MG   C2


MANUSCRIPT  MUSIC


The term HOLOGRAPH has been used to describe original manuscripts
written in the hand of the composer or arranger.  HOLOGRAPH SIGNED
indicated that the manuscript is also signed by the composer.


The term AUTOGRAPH has been employed where the manuscript is written
in the hand of someone other than the composer.


See also:

ALDEN CARPENTER (born Feb. 28, 1876, Park
Ridge, Illinois, U.S.A.): Original MS of/
First version of Last movement of CONCERTINO
for Piano & orchestra (July-Nov, 1915).
Given to the Grainger Museum, Dec. 12, 1939,
by the composer. This first version was
played/by Percy Grainger & the Chicago
Symphony Orchestra (F. Stock) at the 1st per-
formance, early 1916.

Music exercise book [Grainger's title] /

Larger small sketch-book [Grainger's title] /

([44]p. 8 stave No. 22 ms. paper) ; 14 x 19cm. -
Contents: sketches for works of the period 1914-
1917 including the cello sonata, string quartet
and violin concerto, To be sung of a summer's
night on the river, Requiem, and a draft of a
song to Yeats' words 'The lake isle of Innis-
free'. - Ink inscription inside cover: Sketch-

Sept. 1948. - Grainger's inscription on the
package: Sketches for/The Lake Isle of Innis-
free/North Country Sketches (?) /1st cello & piano
sonata/Partsong, to sing on a summer's night/on
the river/Requiem/(Boruch scheomar Gabet)/
(Chamika Hymne).

KNORR, Iwan    1853-1916

MG C2/KNO-2

Unfug' zu drei Stimmen : für das Pianoforte ; canone [= kann ohne!] Schach=Lach=Bach=Fach u. Sachverständnis nicht capirt werden ist aber musikalischer Contra - tiker ein ausserst gefundener Fressen /(Iwan Knorr). - [3]p. on 2 leaves 12 stave ms. paper. - Holograph (ink). - Dedication in Sytterlin script: Schach!/Scinne? lieben [--?] Herrn Carl Klimsch/geboren/von seinem getreuen Cones. [Chess! Given to his dear ...] Herr Carl Klimsch by his faithful comrade. - An elaborate musical joke, in canon form, involving puns and word plays on chess and musical terms. - An approximate translation of the t.p. in English: Nonsense for three voices for the piano : canon : can't be grasped [with- out an] understanding of chess=laughter=Bach= speciality & subject but is a feast for mathematics and counterpoint.

MG C2/KNO-3

Variationen über ein Thema von Karl Klimsch ; für Klavier und Violoncell / comp. von Iwan Knorr. - 1887 März. - 1 unfinished score ([3]p. on 2 folded double leaves 12 stave No. 14 ms. paper) ; 34cm. - For cello and piano. - Holograph signed (ink). - Dedication: Seinem lieben Freunde Herrn Karl Klimsch. - On t.p.: Frankfurt a/Main.

LINDSAY, Nicholas Vachel

MG C2/LIN-1

The Santa Fe trail : script and printed copy / [Nicholas Vachel Lindsay ; annotated by George H. Greenwood]. - 79 numbered p. comprising folded double leaves of 12 stave Monarch Brand No. 10 ms. paper (35cm.) on which are written the words and annotations for spoken performance, interleaved with sheets of blank 12 stave ms. paper (31cm.) on which are pasted the words only, cut out from a published version. - P.41-56 (Through Missouri), cut from a printed work titled The Gospel of Beauty, are taped, as a preface, on p.5. - Holograph[?] (black ink). - Cover p. inscribed: The Santa Fe Trail -/for/ George Greenwood and/Percy Grainger -/from/ Nicholas Vachel Lindsay. - Encl.: photoprint of signed typescript letter on letterhead of The Old National Bank of Spokane, from Greenwood to Grainger, explaining his annotation of the poem. - Transferred from White Plains, 1977.

LISZT, Franz    1811-1886

MG C2/LISZ-8

[Die stille Wasserrose. Sketches / Emanuel Geibel ; Franz Liszt. - 1860 June]. - [2]p. on 1 leaf of ms. paper cut down to 18 staves ; 27 x 35cm. - Holograph (ink) with red pencil annotations. - For voice and piano. - German words. - Encl.: A signed typescript letter (dated February 27, 1933) from the Chief, Division of Music, Library of Congress, to Wallace Goodrich Esq., New England Conservatory, Huntington Ave., Boston. This letter identifies the ms. fragment as a Liszt holograph representing the end (33 measures in the ms., 35 measures in the finished version) of Liszt's song 'Die stille Wasserrose' to a poem by Emanuel Geibel. The letter states that this ms., especially in the accompaniment of the second half, differs considerably from the finished version, and would therefore seem to be a first draft. - Grainger's pencil note on envelope: MS of Franz Liszt given to the Grainger Museum by Mrs. Anna Stovall Lothian, 110 Gainsboro St., Boston, Mass.

MG C2/MEM-1-3-2a

[Memories of New Mexico. Sangre de Cristo]

Sangre de Cristo = (Blood of Christ) : Cruci-
fixion hymn from New Mexico / Natalie Curtis. -
c1921. - 1 piano score/short score sketch. -
2nd expanded version ([3]p. on 1½ folded double
leaves 12 stave G. Schirmer Style No. 2 ms.
paper) ; 35cm. - Holograph signed (blue ink). -
Title on p.[1] : Lenten chant.

Encl.: photoprint of the signed holograph
letter ([3]p. on 2 leaves) from Natalie
Curtis to Percy Grainger, dated March 23rd
1921, which accompanied the ms. - It reads

MEMORIES OF NEW MEXICO

in part: Here is this Hymn of Sorrow and/
Repentance for you to look over at your/
leisure if you really want to, and/not as an
act of friendship ... My reason for first
speaking about these/Mexicanos at all was to
ask if they/would be any good on any kind of/
a reproducing thing. machine.

+ photoprint of signed typescript letter (1
leaf) from Bridgham Curtis to Grainger, dated
March 2, 1925, which reads in part: At
mother's request, I enclose a copy of one of
Natalie's/letters to her which describes a
procession of the Penitentes which/she wit-
nessed on Easter Sunday a few years ago.

+ photoprint of signed holograph letter from
Augusta L. Curtis to Grainger, dated Feb.
28th 1925, which reads in part: ... nothing/
would give me greater/happiness than to have/
her [Natalie Curtis] known to the world/
through your genius-&/loyal friendship/I have
asked Bridgham/to have typewritten a copy/of
a letter she/wrote at the time of/the Peni-
tente procession/giving her impression of
it ...

+ photoprint of the typescript copy of the
letter from Natalie Curtis, dated Easter
Sunday, April 1923, referred to by Bridgham
& Augusta Curtis.

+ photoprint of signed typescript letter (1
leaf) from Bridgham Curtis to Grainger, dated
February 27, 1925, which reads in part: ... I
do not suppose that there can be any possible
ob-/jection on Barrere's part to your orches-
trating and performing the/two pieces.

+ photoprint of the typescript letter (carbon
copy) from Grainger to Barrere, dated March
8, 1925, which reads in part: ... Natalie
Curtis showed/me her arrangements of material
collected in New Mexico and I told/her I
would use 2 or 3 of them as piano pieces and
later on in/chamber arrangements ... I have
made 2 scores for/chamber-orchestra (15
instruments) & propose to perform this ...
wonder whether you would have the least
personal/objection.

The term PHOTOPRINT has been used as the common designation to
describe all photostat copies, dieline print-offs, photographs,
photoreductions and Apeco or Blue Print reproductions of both
manuscript and published music.

FOREWORD

MUSIC is often slightly called a "universal language"; yet, as usually practised, it sometimes seems the most parochial of the arts. People who know of Confucius, Homer and Shakespeare as a matter of course, may never have heard of such towering musical giants of the past as Guillaume de Machaut and William Lawes. Men who respect the great religions, philosophies and architectures of the East, actually laugh when they hear examples of the noblest Oriental art-music. We would not tolerate a theatre that avoided plays dealing with contemporary life, yet we do not protest when our concert halls pass over in silence such outstanding living composers as Arthur Fickenscher and Herman Sandby.

The only music that our concert-goers know really well is that composed between 1700 and 1900 in four or five European countries. No doubt the backwardness of our musical life—as compared with what obtains in the other arts—is partly due to the difficulties formerly presented by the changing musical notation of the earlier European periods, and to the inaccessibility of much extra-European music. But these difficulties are fast being overcome. Inspired students such as Arnold Dolmetsch and Dom Anselm Hughes, O.S.B., have revealed to us the glories of English, French and Netherlands music from, say, 1240 to 1680, while light-shedding albums of gramophone records (such as the "Musik des Orients," published by Curt Lindström, A.G., Kulturabteilung, Berlin) put at our service a wide range of Oriental music (extending from Japan to Tunis), or the significant native music of Negro Africa, Madagascar, and the South Seas. Discerning collectors of folk music, such as Natalie Curtis-Burlin and Madame E. Lineva, have faithfully transcribed, from phonograph records, the improvised folk harmonisations of America, Russia, etc.

The discovery and publication of a large body of harmonically fully developed English music of the late 13th Century ("Worcester Mediæval Harmony," by Dom Anselm Hughes) is particularly interesting in view of the fact that several leading students of musical history (notably Hugo Riemann and Knud Jeppesen) are insisting more and more that the beginning of harmony and polyphony lay in the north-west of Europe—in Scandinavia, England, and the Celtic lands, Flanders, and the North of France.

For permission to use rare or inaccessible manuscripts and publications, Dr. Edgar L. Bainton's and my hearty thanks go to Arnold Dolmetsch (the Dolmetsch Foundation, Haslemere, Surrey, England), Dom Anselm Hughes, O.S.B., the Plainsong and Mediæval Music Society, Nashdown Abbey, Burnham, Bucks, England, Dr. Charles W. Hughes (Hunter College, New York), and Professor Gustave Reese (College of Fine Arts, New York University).

PERCY GRAINGER.

FIRST PROGRAMME. Wednesday, 13th March

(1) TALK (PERCY GRAINGER): "VARIOUS SYSTEMS OF HARMONY."

(2) DEVELOPMENT OF EUROPEAN VOCAL HARMONY.

(a) "Foweles in the Frith," Early English Harmony (about 1210). (Tenor and baritone voices.)

(b) "Alleluya psallat," English, late 13th Century, from "Worcester Mediæval Harmony," by Dom Anselm Hughes. (Six mixed voices and strings.)

(c) "Beata Viscera," English, late 13th Century, from "Worcester Mediæval Harmony," by Dom Anselm Hughes. (Three male voices.)

(d) Palestrina (Italian, 1525-1594): "Mori quasi mio core" (Madrigal for four voices).

(e) Sparre Olsen (Norwegian, b. 1903): "Mountain-Norway." (Mixed voices, strings and harmonium.)

(3) DEVELOPMENT OF EUROPEAN STRING MUSIC.

(a) Guillaume de Machaut (French, 1300-1377): "Rondrau No. 14." (Six strings.)

(b) Josquin des Prez (Netherlands, 1450-1521): "La Bernardina." (Three strings.)

(c) William Byrd (English, 1543-1623): Six-part Fantasy, No. 1. (Six strings.)

(d) William Lawes (English, d. 1645): Six-part Fantasy and Air, No. 1. (Six strings.) (Transcribed from the original manuscript by Arnold Dolmetsch.)

(4) ORIENTAL ART-MUSIC.

"Sekar gadung," Javanese gamelan ensemble, transcribed from a gramophone record by Norman Voeckler and Percy Grainger. (Mezzo-soprano and baritone voices, harmonium, xylophone, metal and wooden marimbas, staff bells.)

(5) DEVELOPMENT OF SONG WITH INSTRUMENTAL ACCOMPANIMENT.

(a) "Aus fahr ich hin" (German song, about 1400). (Baritone voice and viola.)

(b) Lyonel Power (English, b. 1420): "Anima mea liquefacta est." (Contralto voice and three strings.)

(c) Heinrich Finck (German, 1445-1527): "Schoeness Weih." (Tenor voice and three strings.)

(d) Paul Hindemith (German, b. 1895): "Die junge Magd." (Mezzo soprano voice, four strings and harmonium.)

(6) FOLK-HARMONISATIONS AND THEIR INFLUENCE.

(a) "Kindling Wood," from "Great-Russian songs in folk-harmonisation," by E. Lineva. (Three women's voices.)

(b) "The flowers that bloomed in the field," from "Great-Russian songs in folk-harmonisation," by E. Lineva. (Four mixed voices.)

(c) Rachmaninoff (Russian, b. 1872): "Laud ye the name of the Lord," from "Songs of the Church." (Eight mixed voices.)

(d) "Lullaby," from "Negro Folksongs," Hampton Series, noted down by Natalie Curtis-Burlin. (Eight mixed voices.)

(e) "Gawd's a-gwine ter move all de troubles away," from "Negro Folksongs," Hampton Series, noted down by Natalie Curtis-Burlin. (Four male voices.)

(7) EXPERIMENTAL MUSIC.

(a) Claude Le Jeune (Belgian, 1528-1602): "Pretty Swallow." (Six mixed voices.)

(b) Cyril Scott (English, b. 1878): Excerpts from Piano Sonata, Op. 66. (Irregular rhythms.) (Piano solo.)

(c) Cyril Scott (English, b. 1878): Adagio from Quintet for Piano and Strings (gliding intervals).

SECOND PROGRAMME. Saturday, 16th March

(1) TALK (PERCY GRAINGER): "THE GOAL OF MUSICAL PROGRESS."

(2) DEVELOPMENT OF CANON.

(a) Zulu Love Song, transcribed by Natalie Curtis-Burlin. (Two tenor voices.)

(b) Guillaume de Machaut (French, 1300-1377): Ballade No. 17. (Six mixed voices and strings.)

(c) Adrian Willaert (Netherlands, 1480-1562): "O Salutaris Hostia." (Alto and tenor voices and strings.)

(d) Antonio de Cabezón (Spanish, 1510-1566): Prelude in the Dorian mode. (Two violas, two 'cellos.)

(e) William Brade (English, 1560-1630): Allemande. (Six strings.)

(f) Bach (German, 1685-1750): Fugue in A Minor (from "Well-tempered Clavier," Book I). (Four pianists at two pianofortes.)

(3) HYBRID MUSIC (HALF NATIVE, HALF EUROPEAN).

"Mamaphony," transcription from Gramophone record (Madagascar). (Ella and Percy Grainger and baritone voice.)

(4) DEVELOPMENT OF EUROPEAN STRING MUSIC.

(a) John Jenkins (English, 1592-1678): Fantasy for 5 Viols, No. 1, D Major. Transcribed from the original manuscript by Ll. Wyn. Griffith. (Five strings.)

(b) Purcell (English, 1658-1695): Four-part Fantasy, No. 8. (String quartet.)

(c) Herman Sandby (Danish, b. 1881): "Andante Amoroso," from Second String Quartet. (String quartet.)

(5) DEVELOPMENT OF SONG WITH INSTRUMENTAL ACCOMPANIMENT.

(a) Diego Pisador (Spanish, c. 1550): "Paseabase, the Moorish King." (Baritone voice and three strings.)

(b) Guillaume Dufay (Netherlands, about 1400-1474): "Le jour s'endort" ("Softly the day and the season falls asleep"). (Contralto voice and six strings.)

(c) Francesco Corteccia (Italian, d. 1571): "O Glorious Golden Era." (Tenor voice, two violas, two 'cellos.)

(d) Grainger (b. Melbourne, 1882): "Willow, willow." (Baritone voice, violin, viola, two 'cellos and guitar.)

(6) ORIENTALISED EUROPEAN MUSIC.

(a) Debussy (French, 1862-1919): "Pagodes." Pianoforte Solo. Original Version.

(b) Debussy (French, 1862-1919): "Pagodes," arranged for "Tuneful Percussion" (twenty players), by Percy Grainger.

(7) INTERVALS CLOSER THAN THE HALF-TONE.

Arthur Fickenscher (American, b. 1873): "The Seventh Quintet." (Pianoforte and strings.)
DELIUS                                              251                                 FICKENSCHER

261


MG C4


MUSIC, POPULAR (SONGS, ETC.)


This sub-collection consists of items separated out by Ella
Grainger into a box labelled:

To Keep

[E.G.'s remark 1965]

HEREIN

SOME POPULAR

MUSIC

at time of

1st W. War-

much beloved

by P.G. -

(he was in the

Band, then)

pieces such as

"Liberty Bell"

"Nola"

It's a long way

to Tipparany"

& others.

_____________________

"to be kept in
neverent memory
of Percy Grainger"
says Ella Grainger
Oct. 31st. 1965
_____________________


In addition to popular vocal, piano & band music of the war
years, this collection contains items from the 1920's-1940's,
especially excerpts from contemporary musical comedies and
Cuban songs and piano music purchased by Grainger.

All items in this collection are songs for solo voice with
piano accompaniment unless otherwise stated.
262

MG C5

SOCIETY FOR THE PUBLICATION OF AMERICAN MUSIC

CHAMBER MUSIC

All works in this collection, excepting the first, were
published for The Society for the Publication of American
Music by G. Schirmer in New York and bear the plate number
of S.P.A.M.

The items are listed alphabetically by composer under the
years during which they were published.

As a result of the primary chronological arrangement an
extra element, denoting the season during which published,
is included in the classification number e.g. MG C5 1/MAS-12
MG C5 8/HILL-1

See also MG CI/LOE-3.

271


      MG   D1


     MRS DAHL'S COLLECTION


Lisken Dahl (née Ström) is assumed to have been a relative of
Ella Grainger, possibly a sister-in-law to Ella's foster mother
'Tant Ström'.


This small collection, generally comprising popular vocal and
piano music published chiefly in Stockholm, Copenhagen and
Oslo, was originally found in a package posted to Grainger by
a Mrs H.P. Hansen from Herning in Denmark.


The package was inscribed by Ella Grainger in pencil:


    Mrs Dahl's
    gift
    of OLD-FASHIONED
    piano music
    Music given to E.G. & P.G.
    by Mrs Dahl -
    July 1952
272

ZIEHRER, C M 1843-1922

MG D1/ZIE-1

Blumen-Polka : polka francaise ; op. 316 / af C.M. Ziehrer. - Kjöbenhavn : Wilhelm Hansen, [s.a.]. - 3p. ; 32cm. - For piano. - Pl.no.: 9331. - Cover signed in pencil: frk. Agnes Strom.
"Highfield"
Wallace St.
Toorak.

Dear Miss Francis.

I am very sorry
that I will not be able to spend next
Thursday evening with you, but
mother does not allow me to go out
at night by myself. I must try to
see Miss Fletcher before she leaves
Melbourne, will you kindly re-
member me to her.

Thank you for your kind
invitation &

Believe me
Truly Yours
Percy.

March 19th 1895.

(Top left small handwritten number: 1395)
275


MG F1


WINIFRED FRANCIS' MUSIC


Note in the Personal Column of the Argus 16 March 1905:

Many will learn with regret of the death
of Mr. Henry Francis, J.P., chemist, of
Bourke and Collins streets. The deceased
had been suffering from a complication of
disorders for some months. He came to
Victoria in 1852, and, after a few weeks at
the diggings at Bendigo, he began business
as a chemist at Kyneton, but soon moved
to Melbourne, where he established the
business in Bourke-street which he has car-
ried on for 45 years. He was one of the
founders of the Pharmaceutical Society in
1866, and occupied the presidential chair in
1884, and for fourteen years he was one of
the examiners of the Pharmacy Board. Mr.
Francis in 1862 married Miss Grace Laven-
der, the daughter of a police magistrate.
Mrs. Francis survives her husband, and he
leaves a family of three daughters and two
sons. The elder son succeeds his father in
the business.

Winifred Francis was one of these daughters. She remained unmarried
and lived at "Chediston", the family home in Fulton Street, East St.
Kilda, where Henry Francis died.

An amateur pianist, she was a friend of the Graingers in the early
1890s when Percy was quite small. The friendship must have continued
over the years as Percy visited her in 1938 when he came to Australia
for the opening of the Grainger Museum and wrote to her from Honolulu
in late February 1939.

Her letter of 11 June [1938?] to Professor Heinze records part of her
gift to the Museum:

.... you might be
wondering what the old book of music
(1822) with criticisms etc. of Percy Grainger in it
was sent for. I sent it to see if it would
be of any use for the Grainger Museum
on account of the quaint old songs in it.
Also thought the Grainger cuttings might
be of interest for the Museum ...
276

BEETHOVEN, Ludwig van 1770-1827

281

MG  J1

JOHN GRAINGER'S MUSIC

Percy Grainger's father, John Harry Grainger, was a very
gifted and highly respected architect. He was born in
London in 1854 and in 1877 he emigrated to Australia where
he became Assistant Architect and Engineer to the govern-
ment of South Australia. It was in Adelaide that he met
and married Rosa (Rose) Annie Aldridge. In 1880, his
winning entry, with J.S. Jenkins, in a design competition for
the Princes Bridge brought him to Melbourne, where Percy
Grainger was born in 1882.

In 1897, John Grainger was appointed Chief Architect to the
government of Western Australia. He retired in 1905 due to
ill health and later returned to Melbourne, setting up a
private business known as Grainger and Little. He died in
Melbourne in 1917.

John Grainger was a fine amateur painter and his interest
and activity in the fields of literature, art and music had
a profound influence on his son. Although John Grainger
did not play a musical instrument, he was actively involved
in music, establishing one of the first string quartets in
Adelaide. In Perth, he sat on the committee of the Perth
Amateur Orchestral Society and was instrumental in
encouraging performances of his son's early orchestral
works.
Here is a transcription of all the visible text on the page. Where the script is too faint or unclear I have marked it as [illegible/faint].

ROYAL STUDIO
PERTH

with dearest love,
John S. Grainger  7/7/98

MG Q1

AUNTIE JACK'S MUSIC

This small collection of music for, or arranged for, the piano was given to Grainger for his museum by A. Marie Quesnel.

Grainger's mother Rose came from a family of nine children. In 1880 her eldest brother, George Sydney Aldridge, married into the MacFie family. His wife Marion ('May') was the eldest of seven children. Her youngest sister was Anna Maria MacFie who later married Reginald Quesnel.

It was Anna Maria Quesnel (née MacFie) who was known affectionately by the Aldridge and MacFie families as 'Jack' and whilst strictly speaking she was not an aunt of Percy Grainger, he called her 'Aunty Jack'.

'Aunty Jack' stayed with Percy Grainger's parents in the early 1880s when they were living in Brighton, Melbourne. After Percy and Rose had gone to live in Frankfurt-am-Main, 'Aunty Jack' continued to be a good friend to Percy's father John Grainger, sharing with him an interest in architecture and music.

'Aunty Jack' was apparently a very talented singer and pianist. On 16 June 1896, John Grainger wrote to his son Percy:

    Aunty Jack is just now playing a Sonata in A of Mozarts [sic] very nicely in the other room, she has much improved during the last year. She is a good conscientious woman in all she does and especially so in music. very hard working at it, but a bit hampered as May & George [Aldridge] dont [sic] like "Strumming" as they are pleased to term it ... I think Auntie Jack's voice appeals more to my sense than anyone else I ever heard [There is] something sympathetic in it, which touches me whatever she may sing, in fact it is the only voice who [which] gives me real great pleasure in listening to it. and she sings her songs with great feeling.

It would appear that 'Aunty Jack' left Reginald Quesnel some years after their marriage and that she moved to Sydney and became a singing teacher. In 1908 she went to London where she met many well known musical people. She also visited Paris but it is not clear whether this was in order to perform or study.

1. British Australasian 6 August 1908.
292
293

MG  R1


ROBERT HAMILTON RUSSELL'S MUSIC
_______________________________


Dr. Robert Hamilton Russell, born in 1860, trained under Lister at King's College
Hospital, London. He emigrated to Australia in the late 1880s and became a
friend of the Graingers in Melbourne just before Percy's parents separated in 1891.

Hamilton Russell's reputation as an eminent surgeon in Melbourne meant that he was
in great demand. Whilst having a private practice in the city, he also, at
various times practiced at the Children's Hospital, the Eye and Ear Hospital and
the Queen Victoria. He is best known, however, for his association with the
Alfred Hospital where he was a senior surgeon from 1901 to 1919 and Dean of the
Clinical School until 1929. He has been described by one author as 'the Alfred's
favourite son'.

Hamilton Russell was also an accomplished amateur pianist with a broad knowledge
of music. He took it upon himself to see that the young Grainger heard as many
different types of music as possible, taking Percy to orchestral concerts and
recitals. He thus had a profound influence on the formation of Grainger's early
musical tastes.

Grainger considered that Hamilton Russell's piano playing was

'a real boon to me, for it had a lovable richness & soft
fullness... that I seemed never to have heard before'.2

He also attributed his understanding and love of Schumann's compositions to
Russell's influence.

Grainger's friendship with Dr. Hamilton Russell lasted until the latter's untimely
death in 1933. In 1924 when Grainger was returning to the U.S.A. after visiting
his relatives in Australia, he shared a cabin with Hamilton Russell on board the
S.S. 'Manganui'. On his 1926 concert tour of Australia, Grainger stayed with
Hamilton Russell in Cliveden Mansions whilst in Melbourne. Cliveden Mansions had
been reconstructed many years before by Grainger's father John Harry Grainger and
was on the site now occupied by the Hilton Hotel.

Several painters, sculptors and photographers have made studies of Hamilton Russell
and Hamilton Russell House at the Alfred Hospital was named in memory of his
service to that hospital.


1. Ann M. Mitchell, The Hospital South of the Yarra (Alfred Hospital,
   Melbourne 1977), pp. 275-277.

2. Percy A. Grainger, The Aldridge-Grainger-Ström Saga (Grainger Museum),
   photoprint of ms., p. 199.
This "counterfeit presentment" to
my very dear friend Percy Grainger

R. Hamilton Russell
297

MG S1  &  MG S2


ANTONIA  SAWYER'S  MUSIC


Forenote to Antonia Sawyer, Songs at Twilight (The Devin-Adair Company,
New York), [1939]:


Dear Antonia: -

    Please forgive me if I say that, of the many fine and notable
things you have done, this splendid story of your life, so arrest-
ingly told, seems to me the most valuable of them all, for a fine
record of a fine life always seems to me to have a deeper, more
lasting value than the life itself. You see, I am incorrigibly fond
of art and too often, I fear, look upon life as a mere 'feeder' to
art, like the advocate of canals in England, nearly a hundred
years ago, who when asked by an opponent what he thought
God had made the rivers for, replied, "to feed navigable canals."

    Love, thanks and admiration,

                Percy.


    I am trusting Mr. Grainger's generous nature to forgive the
liberty I take with what was written by him as a personal note
to his long-time manager and friend. It is a fitting overture to
this volume. For he speaks by authority as an eminent citizen
of that world state of harmony, with its world language, whose
trumpets yet may overthrow the political, lingual and racial
walls that unhappily divide humanity and threaten the destruc-
tion of civilization.

    In that world of music, Antonia Sawyer Miner has lived all
her days. As a child, she came into it singing from out a sing-
ing family in a singing state, when the musical festivals of
Maine were of nation-wide renown, and Nordica and Eames
were the bright particular stars of a large company of gifted
musicians down East. She was one of them and knew
most of them, who live and sing again in the memories she has
fondly transcribed on these pages.

    As a choir singer in Boston and New York, as concert singer
throughout the country, as teacher, as manager of some of the
most distinguished artists of her time, her life was woven into
the musical history of America in the past three score years.

    *  *  *  *  *

    A woman with an open heart, a friend with a helping hand,
the warmth of her voice and the beauty of her speech, her
gracious presence, these themselves are music to all who have
the happiness to know Antonia Sawyer.

                                James Morgan

                                Boston.
Morris
N.Y.
307

APPENDIX 1

PIECES INSCRIBED TO THE GRAINGERS

All pieces listed bear hand-written inscriptions, usually the composer's holograph but occasionally in the hand of a donor where the item was received as a gift from someone other than the composer.

The inscriptions are chiefly to Percy Grainger. Where a piece is not inscribed solely to Percy Grainger, an abbreviated note is made in square brackets. [P.G. & E.G.], for example, indicates that the inscription is to Percy and Ella Grainger.

The name of the inscriber is listed in a form which provides full identification and is therefore not necessarily the precise signature as found on the actual item.

- upload a higher-resolution or closer crop of the page, or

APPENDIX 3                                      314

PIECES DEDICATED TO GRAINGER

Items listed from Grainger's published music collection (those with classification numbers beginning
MG C1) usually contain a formal printed note of dedication to Grainger. In the manuscript and photo-
printed music collections (MG C2 and MG C3), however, the dedication more often takes the form of an
inscription or letter to Grainger in the composer's holograph.

The dedications are chiefly to Percy Grainger. Where a work is not dedicated solely to Percy Grainger,
an abbreviated note is made in square brackets.

The name of the composer making the dedication to Grainger is given in a form which provides fullest
possible identification.

APPENDIX 14                                     320


CORRESPONDENCE


This appendix lists correspondence found enclosed in items in Grainger's collection of music by other
composers, generally comprising signed typescript and holograph letters from composers or publishers
to Percy Grainger.  In these cases, the documents have been transferred to Grainger's Correspondence
Collection and a photoprint only is now stored with the particular piece of music in which the original
was found.

Where the correspondence is not addressed to Grainger, however, the letters remain with the music in
which they were found.  This also applies, of course, where a letter is actually written on a particular
score itself rather than on a separate leaf attached to it.

APPENDIX 15                                      321


ROSE GRAINGER'S MUSIC


Rose Grainger, the mother of Australian-born pianist/composer Percy Grainger, was born in Adelaide in
1861. At the age of 19 she married the architect and civil engineer, John H. Grainger and they moved to
Melbourne where Percy Grainger was born in 1882.


Her maiden name was Aldridge. Percy's Grainger's full name was George Percy Grainger, but he later took
the stage name of Percy Aldridge Grainger, thus incorporating his mother's maiden name.


Rose Grainger was a very talented and competent pianist. She was Percy's first piano teacher and he
relates how she 'sat beside her son (aged 5-10) for 2 or more hours daily while he practised the piano.'


In the early 1890s, John and Rose Grainger separated. Rose supported herself and her son by earning a
living as a piano teacher in Melbourne. When Rose and Percy Grainger moved to Germany to further his
musical studies, she helped to support them both by working in Frankfurt-am-Main as an English tutor.
Financial support also came from John Grainger, who regularly sent sums of money to assist with his
son's education. Rose's interests in music, literature, the theatre and the Australian and American
countryside greatly influenced her son. Percy Grainger says of his mother:

'To this her early devotion to me and to art alike I owe the greatest part of
whatever artistic facility and concentration I may possess...'


Percy Grainger and his mother had an unusually close relationship and her death in 1922 left him grief-
stricken and lonely. Rose Grainger died tragically at the age of 60 when she threw herself from an
office window of a multi-storey building in New York, her mental condition resulting from a long and
harrowing illness.


Unlike the other small personal collections incorporated into Grainger's collection of music by other
composers, Rose Grainger's music was not separated out into a distinct unit but integrated with
Grainger's own collection.


Most pieces listed are either signed by Rose Grainger or bear explanatory inscriptions by Percy Grainger
identifying them as Rose's music.


MG C1/ALT-1                         MG C1/MUS-3
